reasoning: ACLED data shows that Malaysia experienced only about 1 riot in the 360 days leading up to April 2025, setting a baseline value of roughly 1.083. The resolution criteria require more than 10 times this baseline value in a 30-day window, which means 11 or more riots must occur within a single month. Historically, violent unrest categorized as "Riots" (which entails violence and destruction by a mob, distinct from peaceful protests) is extremely rare in Malaysia. Current news reflects political stability and even a move by Prime Minister Anwar Ibrahim to drop previous protest restrictions, significantly reducing the chances of police-protester clashes escalating into organized riots. The required spike to 11+ riots would necessitate an unpredictable, severe breakdown of public order (such as severe race riots or a massive political crisis). No recent news from late March or April 2025 suggests any ongoing unrest, virtually ruling out the April and May resolution dates. The Sabah state elections are coming up by the end of 2025, but Malaysian elections historically do not trigger double-digit numbers of violent riots. Therefore, the probability of exceeding the threshold rests solely on black-swan events, meaning the forecasts are anchored strictly at the lowest allowed probabilities, scaling marginally up to 0.04 for the late 2025 dates to account for unpredictable long-term uncertainty.
